subject: geography
jamb_high_priority_topics: The Earth and its Planetary System

subtopics.title: The Solar System
common_misconceptions: There are more than 8 planets (Pluto was reclassified)
The earth is perfectly spherical (it's an oblate spheroid)
subtopics.teaching_note: The solar system: Sun at center, 8 planets orbiting. Terrestrial planets (Mercury, Venus, Earth, Mars) - rocky, inner. Gas giants (Jupiter, Saturn) and ice giants (Uranus, Neptune) - outer. Earth: third planet from sun, average distance 149.6 million km (1 AU), only known planet with life. Earth's shape: oblate spheroid (flattened at poles, bulging at equator). Equatorial diameter = 12,756 km, Polar diameter = 12,714 km.

subtopics.title: Rotation and Revolution of the Earth
common_misconceptions: Seasons are caused by distance from sun (caused by tilt)
The earth's axis is perpendicular to orbital plane (tilted at 23.5°)
All countries have 12 hours day and night year-round (only at equator)
subtopics.teaching_note: Rotation: earth spins on axis (west to east) → day and night. Period = 24 hours. Revolution: earth orbits sun → year. Period = 365.25 days (leap year every 4 years). Tilt of axis = 23.5° → seasons. Summer solstice (June 21): North Pole tilts toward sun. Winter solstice (Dec 22): North Pole tilts away. Equinoxes (March 21, Sept 23): equal day and night worldwide. Time zones: 24 zones, 15° longitude each. Nigeria: GMT+1 (one time zone).

subtopics.title: Latitude and Longitude
common_misconceptions: Latitude lines run north-south (they run east-west)
Longitude lines are parallel (they converge at poles)
The Prime Meridian passes through Paris (it passes through Greenwich, London)
subtopics.teaching_note: Latitude: angular distance north/south of Equator (0° to 90°N/S). Key parallels: Equator 0°, Tropic of Cancer 23.5°N, Tropic of Capricorn 23.5°S, Arctic Circle 66.5°N, Antarctic Circle 66.5°S. Longitude: angular distance east/west of Prime Meridian (0° to 180°E/W). Prime Meridian through Greenwich, London. Important longitudes: 0° (Greenwich), 15°E (Central European Time), 180° (International Date Line). Nigeria: approximately 4°N to 14°N, 3°E to 14°E. Great circle: shortest distance between two points on earth's surface.

subtopics.title: Calculations on Latitude and Longitude
common_misconceptions: 1° of longitude = same distance everywhere (varies by latitude)
Crossing date line adds time (changes date)
subtopics.teaching_note: Key formulas: Distance along meridian = difference in latitude × 111 km. Distance along parallel = difference in longitude × 111 km × cos(latitude). Time: earth rotates 360° in 24 hours → 15° per hour → 1° per 4 minutes. Local time difference = longitude difference × 4 minutes. GMT+1 for Nigeria. International Date Line: 180° longitude, cross westward → add a day; cross eastward → subtract a day.

subtopics.title: Types and Uses of Maps
common_misconceptions: Large scale means large area (NO - large scale = large detail, small area)
All maps are drawn to scale
North is always at the top of a map
subtopics.teaching_note: Map = representation of earth's surface on flat paper. Scale: ratio between map distance and ground distance. Large scale: 1:10,000 (small area, more detail). Small scale: 1:1,000,000 (large area, less detail). Representative Fraction (RF): 1:50,000 means 1cm on map = 50,000cm (500m) on ground. Conventional signs: standardized symbols for features. Grid references: 4-figure (area) and 6-figure (precise point). Nigeria's topographic maps produced by Surveyor General's Office.

subtopics.title: Map Interpretation
common_misconceptions: Intervisibility only considers direct line of sight (consider contour heights too)
All drainage patterns are the same
subtopics.teaching_note: Relief: shape of land surface shown by contours, spot heights, trigonometric stations. Intervisibility: can one point be seen from another? Check if contour between them is higher than line of sight. Gradient = vertical interval / horizontal equivalent (VI/HE), often expressed as 1:X or ratio. Cross section: profile of land along a line. Vertical exaggeration = vertical scale / horizontal scale. Settlement patterns: dispersed, nucleated, linear. Drainage patterns: dendritic, radial, trellis, rectangular, centripetal.
